title: ReAct Failure Modes
concept: Reasoning drifting into hallucination, forgetting the main goal, and repeating the same thought-action loop.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, what did a good ReAct system prompt teach the model to do?

options.text: Loop through Thought, Action, and Observation steps in order
Answer instantly without thinking or using tools
Only draw pictures instead of text

exercises.explain: A ReAct prompt structure teaches the base model the pattern: think (Thought), do something (Action), then read the result (Observation), and repeat until done.

cards.title: Even Smart Agents Get Stuck
cards.body: A ReAct agent thinks and acts step by step. But sometimes it wanders off, forgets what it was doing, or gets trapped saying the same thing forever.

cards.title: What Are Failure Modes?
cards.body: Failure modes are the common ways a ReAct agent breaks. The big three: making up facts, forgetting its goal, and looping on repeat.

cards.title: A Lost Hiker
cards.body: Picture a hiker on a trail. They might invent a shortcut that isn't real, forget where they were headed, or keep circling the same tree over and over.

cards.title: Stuck in a Loop
cards.body: An agent thinks 'I should search for the price,' searches, gets a result, then thinks 'I should search for the price' again... and again. It never uses what it found.

cards.title: Myth: More Steps Always Help
cards.body: Not true! Letting an agent run forever can make it drift into made-up facts. Good agents have step limits and reminders of the goal.

cards.title: Healthy vs. Broken Loop
cards.body: A healthy loop moves toward the goal. A broken loop drifts, forgets, or repeats.

left.text: Each step uses the last result and gets closer to the answer.

right.text: Steps ignore results, invent facts, or repeat endlessly.

exercises.prompt: An agent's Thought says 'The capital of Australia is Sydney' — but it never checked and it's wrong. Which failure mode is this?
exercises.explain: When reasoning states something confidently without checking, it can drift into hallucination — an invented fact that sounds right but isn't. (The real capital is Canberra!)

exercises.prompt: True or False: An agent that keeps repeating the exact same Thought and Action without using the Observation is stuck in a loop.
exercises.explain: Repeating the same thought-action over and over while ignoring the observation is a classic looping failure — the agent never moves forward.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about a common ReAct failure.
exercises.sentence: When an agent gets distracted and stops working toward its task, it is forgetting the main goal.
exercises.explain: Forgetting the main goal means the agent loses track of what it was asked to do and wanders off task.

exercises.prompt: Which agent Thought was AI-generated and shows hallucination (a made-up 'fact')?

options.text: Thought: The user's file has exactly 4,217 lines, so I'll skip to the end. (never opened the file)
Thought: I haven't opened the file yet, so I should read it first.
Thought: The search returned no results, so I'll try different keywords.

exercises.explain: Option A invents a precise detail (4,217 lines) it could not know without checking — that confident, unchecked claim is hallucination.

exercises.prompt: An agent is asked to 'find today's weather, then suggest an outfit.' It searches the weather, gets '15°C and rainy,' but then keeps searching the weather again and again, never suggesting an outfit. Which two failure modes are happening?

options.text: Repeating the same loop AND forgetting the main goal
Forgetting the main goal AND answering too fast
Hallucination AND a perfectly healthy loop

exercises.explain: The agent repeats the identical search instead of using the result (looping), and it never gets to the outfit suggestion (forgetting the main goal). Good ReAct design fixes this with step limits and goal reminders — a big reason knowing these failure modes helps you build agents that actually finish the job.
